cataloging_subject.label: Proverbs from Harley MS 3362 (Wright's 1854 selection)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact sequence of 31 Middle English proverbial sayings printed from British Library Harley MS 3362 on p.309 of The Retrospective Review in May 1854 and encoded as CME00128. It is modeled as an anonymous manuscript-specific compilation or editorial selection of distinct sayings, not as one stable authorial poem, not as all proverbial material in Harley MS 3362, and not as one DIMEV work. The manuscript witness, Wright's edition, bound serial volume, scan, U-M encoding, and repository XML remain separate lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Proverbs from Harley MS 3362 (Wright's 1854 selection)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.editor: Thomas Wright (attributed by DIMEV from the printed signature W.)
catalog_summary.date: Compilation no later than the fifteenth-century witness; individual sayings may be older; first known publication of this selection May 1854
catalog_summary.region: England; exact compilation place unknown; Harley MS 3362 scribal language localized by DIMEV/LALME to Ely

assertion.notes: The witness and editor qualifier distinguishes this exact 31-saying selection from other proverb compilations; it is not a manuscript title.

notes: This is the generic printed heading, not evidence for an original manuscript rubric.

titles.value: Whyl the dogge gnawyth, the cat wolde ete

agents.name: Thomas Wright
notes: The page is signed only W.; DIMEV bibliography entry Wright1854b attributes the item to Thomas Wright as editor. He is not a medieval author.

scope.description: The constituent sayings, which may have multiple independent origins

assertion.notes: Anonymous is a display responsibility, not a claim that one unidentified person authored all 31 sayings.

Compiler is the most useful role for a manuscript-specific sequence of independently circulating sayings, although the precise boundary between manuscript compilation and Wright's editorial selection remains unresolved.
The print signs the item W.; DIMEV expands this through bibliography entry Wright1854b.

scope.description: The represented readings selected from Harley MS 3362

assertion.notes: The exact editorial workflow and use of any intermediary transcript are not stated on the page.

date_statements.display: No later than the fifteenth-century witness (1400–1499); exact compilation date unknown

assertion.notes: The witness supplies a terminus ante quem for the represented sequence, not an origin date for every proverb. Wright's end-of-century wording is narrower than the current British Library's whole-century date.

scope.description: The represented sayings' survival in the witness, not a confirmed autonomous boundary for Wright's exact 31-item selection

notes: This confirms that the sayings represented by the cataloged selection survive in a fifteenth-century witness. It does not confirm that Wright's exact 31-item selection already existed as an autonomous manuscript unit. Individual sayings have separate transmission histories and may be older.

date_statements.display: May 1854 (first known publication of this exact selection)

assertion.notes: This does not claim first publication of every independently circulating proverb.

places.label: Ely, Cambridgeshire, England
notes: Used for DIMEV's manuscript-level LALME/eLALME localization, not as a confirmed place of composition, compilation, or copying.

assertion.notes: The witness is English and the sayings are Middle English; the precise compilation place remains unknown.
DIMEV reports LALME/eLALME localization 1.112 Ely for the manuscript. This is a scribal-language association and is not transferred to authorial origin or place of compilation.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English in the heading and headnote belongs to the 1854 editorial layer; the cataloged sayings are Middle English.

form_statements.description: The selection combines separately indexed proverbial couplets with one-line sayings and readings that DIMEV classifies as prose or prosified. The 33 XML L elements in one LG reproduce print display units but do not establish that every item is verse.

subject_statements.label: Conduct of life and practical wisdom
Fortune, divine disposition, necessity, and appetite
Social observation, envy, self-interest, and unequal reward

summaries.value: Thirty-one compact sayings offer practical and social observations about youthful sanctity and later vice, silence and gossip, hardship, experience, memory, prudence, envy, necessity, appetite, self-interest, unequal reward, and divine disposition. Familiar images include the tongue that breaks bone, the absent cat, the burned person's fear of fire, God disposing what humans propose, the field's eye and wood's ear, the bird in hand, the cat that knows whose beard it licks, and the dog and cat competing for food.

The 1854 page contains 31 visually delimited sayings: 29 one-line items and two two-line units, 'Tunge brekyth bon / thow hyrself have non' and 'Labbe hyt whyste / and owt yt muste.' CME00128 serializes the 33 display lines as L elements in one LG. Six sayings end in ellipses; only the cat-beard item is explicitly labeled incomplete by DIMEV.

The record preserves the exact 31-saying printed sequence as one compilation while separately linking only secure or materially important DIMEV identities. It does not explode all 33 XML lines into independent works, treat the 16 DIMEV Harley witness-index entries as target items, or force a single verse classification onto prose and prosified readings.

extent.description: The complete selection has 31 visually delimited sayings in one unnumbered section. Print and XML contain 33 display-line units because two sayings have indented continuation lines. The XML has one LG and 33 L elements, but these are representation metrics rather than a secure claim of 33 verse lines; DIMEV classifies only a subset as verse and identifies other exact Harley readings as prose or prosified.

content_parts.label: Thirty-one-saying proverb sequence
content_parts.summary: The complete manuscript-derived sequence printed under the heading PROVERBS, from 'Ȝoung seynt, old devyl' through 'Whyl the dogge gnawyth, the cat wolde ete.'

locators.value: p.309, from the centered PROVERBS heading below the preceding item's rule through signed W. and the rule at the page foot
PDF page 759 / Internet Archive leaf n758
opening proverb run, provisionally ff.2r–5; secure target readings at f.2v and f.4v; exact continuous boundaries unresolved
DIV1/LG[1], 33 L display units representing 31 visually delimited sayings

notes: Complete means complete relative to Wright's printed selection. The exact manuscript surface inventory and the original compilation boundary remain unresolved.
The six printed ellipses are preserved without assigning a cause.

related_works.title: Tongue breaketh bone
notes: Secure exact two-line target match at Harley f.2v, column 1. DIMEV also records another Harley occurrence at f.21; the target text matches the f.2v reading.

related_works.title: A man proposeth, God disposeth
notes: Secure exact target match at Harley f.2v, column 2. DIMEV represents it as a couplet even though Wright prints the two clauses on one line.

related_works.title: Well wot the cat whose beard he licks
notes: Secure target match at current f.4v, column 1; DIMEV explicitly calls the Harley reading incomplete. Förster cites Kemble's old fol.6, showing an unreconciled foliation difference.

related_works.title: The wood hath ears, the field sight
notes: The exact target line is associated by DIMEV as a prosified Harley version rather than an active verse witness. Its ghost note says f.4, a commented legacy witness says f.5, and Förster cites old fol.5; the locus systems remain unresolved.

related_works.title: While the hound gnaweth the bone
notes: DIMEV quotes the exact full final target line as a prosified Harley version at f.5; a commented legacy witness and Förster's Kemble citation use fol.7. It is not promoted to a secure active verse witness.

related_works.title: The cat will fish eat
notes: DIMEV indexes the tail 'the cat would eat…' at Harley f.5 as the incomplete first line of a different proverb record. This overlaps the full final saying's prosified association with record-6523; both identities are preserved without silently choosing one.

evidence.summary: Primary evidence for language, heading, headnote, incipit, explicit, 33 display-line units, six ellipses, subjects, and the signed closer.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from the May 1854 Retrospective Review PROVERBS item and complete scan.
evidence.summary: Supports the exact printed sequence, 31 visual units, line indentation, ellipses, edition heading and headnote, signature W., p.309 spatial boundary, issue and volume date, and direct manuscript claim.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from the British Library record for Harley MS 3362.
evidence.summary: Supports shelfmark, record identity, fifteenth-century date, English origin, broad contents and opening incipit, material, extent, script, and the need to keep whole-codex scope separate from the target selection.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from DIMEV v1.0 target-relevant Harley MS 3362 records and Wright1854b bibliography.
evidence.summary: Supports Wright's attributed editorial role, item-level identifiers and titles, secure and qualified loci, verse/prose/prosified form distinctions, incomplete-fragment status, Ely manuscript localization, the 16 whole-codex witness entries, and conflicting legacy loci.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from Max Förster, 'Frühmittelenglische Sprichwörter' (1902).
evidence.summary: Supplies later comparative evidence for the tongue, cat-beard, cat-would-eat, need, field, and dog sayings and preserves Kemble's old folio 3–7 citations as a separate, unreconciled foliation system.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage and access evidence for CME00128.
evidence.summary: Supports the U-M identity, source-edition linkage, complete serial and supporting-study scans, page mappings, current negative manuscript-image finding, physical consultation route, and whole-object reproduction route.

open_questions.question: What exact manuscript surfaces and sequence boundary correspond to Wright's 31 sayings?
next_steps: Inspect complete color images of the opening proverb run and make a line-by-line concordance.
Keep secure DIMEV f.2v and f.4v locators distinct from qualified ff.4–5 prose/prosified associations.
Reconcile current BL/DIMEV foliation with Förster's Kemble-derived old fols.3, 5, 6, and 7 before assigning a continuous target range.

open_questions.question: How much of the ordering and boundary belongs to the medieval manuscript compilation, and how much to Wright's editorial selection?
next_steps: Compare the exact manuscript order with the printed page once images are available.
Retain compilation as the cataloging unit while qualifying the preferred title with Wright's 1854 selection.
Do not infer continuity solely from the compact print layout or XML LG.

open_questions.question: Which remaining sayings have secure independent index identities, and how should the prosified and overlapping final items be modeled?
next_steps: Add item-level identities only where boundaries and correspondence are secure and useful for discovery.
Preserve the record-6523 prosified full-line association and record-5230 incomplete-fragment overlap together.
Do not map the target shoe/foot saying to DIMEV record 6520 because DIMEV explicitly rejects a Harley witness for 4079.3.

open_questions.question: What manuscript or editorial condition does each of the six printed ellipses represent?
next_steps: Compare each elliptical saying with manuscript images and later transcriptions.
Retain DIMEV's incomplete label only for the cat-beard saying unless another source establishes the other causes.
Do not call the remaining ellipses manuscript damage, illegibility, or omission without evidence.

open_questions.question: When and where was this exact sequence compiled, and does the manuscript-level Ely localization apply uniformly to the target run?
next_steps: Use the fifteenth-century witness date only as first attestation and a compilation terminus.
Keep Ely as a tentative scribal dialect region rather than a place of composition or compilation.
Consult complete manuscript images and current linguistic scholarship before narrowing date or origin.

notes: The preferred title is supplied for disambiguation; the generic printed heading is retained separately as PROVERBS.
No single DIMEV or IMEV/NIMEV identifier is assigned to the 31-saying whole. Related-work identifiers apply only to specific sayings or materially overlapping fragments.
The manuscript witness date, English origin, and Ely scribal-language localization are not silently transferred to every proverb's composition.

The source page is shared: the target occupies the middle and lower portion of printed p.309, not the whole physical page. Complete source coverage means the entire one-page Wright item from heading through final rule.
A complete manuscript facsimile should cover the whole current physical object and map the work's opening-folio surfaces separately.
